metadata.title: Temporal Pitch Perception of Multi-Channel Stimuli by Cochlear-Implant Users
metadata.journal: Journal of the Association for Research in Otolaryngology
metadata.note: Psychophysical study with 8 MED-EL cochlear implant users. Explores feasibility of fine structure processing (FSP) strategies for pitch perception by presenting temporal fine structure (TFS) information to multiple electrodes.

claims.claim_text: Cochlear implant users do not extract the fundamental frequency (F0) from multi-channel stimuli with harmonically related rates; mixed-rate stimuli produce pitch ranks between those of the lowest and second-lowest same-rate stimuli rather than matching the F0 rate

axis_proposal.definition: Subjective pitch rank on ordinal scale from pitch ranking procedure (MidPoint Comparison method)
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Psychophysical measure of perceived pitch height in cochlear implant users

statistics.note: Pitch rank for [1234]SD (mixed-rate: 100, 200, 300, 400 pps on e1-e4) significantly higher than [1111]SD (same-rate 100 pps), but lower than [2222]SD (same-rate 200 pps). All participants except M014 produced pitch rank between [1111]SD and [2222]SD.

methodology.task: Pitch ranking using MidPoint Comparison (MPC) procedure with 'best of three' modification
methodology.control_conditions: Same-rate stimuli (100, 200, 300, 400 pps on all electrodes) as comparison
methodology.sample_size: 8 MED-EL CI users (2 women, 6 men, age 53-83 years)
methodology.participant_characteristics: Postlingually deaf, using MED-EL device for at least 2 years, fitted with FSP strategies

claims.claim_text: Between-electrode delays significantly affect pitch perception in multi-rate stimuli, with long-delay (LD) conditions producing higher pitch ranks than short-delay (SD) conditions

axis_proposal.definition: Subjective pitch rank on ordinal scale
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Psychophysical measure of perceived pitch height

statistics.note: For multi-rate stimulus [1234], all participants except MED06 and possibly MED05 assigned higher pitch ranks to LD than SD version. Effect highly significant at group level. For same-rate [1111], difference not significant at corrected level (p = 0.016, d = 1.1).

methodology.task: Pitch ranking comparing short-delay (100 µs) vs long-delay (maximized IPI) conditions

claims.claim_text: Pitch ranks for same-rate stimuli increase with pulse rate up to 200-300 pps, then plateau, consistent with upper limit of temporal pitch perception in cochlear implants

axis_proposal.definition: Subjective pitch rank on ordinal scale
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Psychophysical measure of perceived pitch height

statistics.note: Pitch increases markedly with pulse rate from 100 to 200 pps, less so with further increases to 300 and especially 400 pps. Plateau starting at 200 or 300 pps depending on participant. Consistent with literature on upper limit of pitch for single-pulse-per-period (SPP) pulse trains.

methodology.task: Pitch ranking of same-rate stimuli (100, 200, 300, 400 pps on all four electrodes)

claims.claim_text: Mixed-rate stimuli produce less reliable pitch percepts than same-rate stimuli, with larger between-run variance in pitch rankings

axis_proposal.definition: Standard deviation of pitch ranks across multiple runs, indicating reliability/consistency of pitch percept
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Measure of pitch percept clarity and consistency in cochlear implant users

statistics.note: Between-run variance averaged across listeners: SD = 1.4, 0.8, 0.8, 0.8 for same-rate SD conditions vs SD = 1.7, 2.0, 1.8, 1.4 for mixed-rate conditions. Mixed-rate stimuli had less well-defined pitch than same-rate stimuli.

methodology.task: Pitch ranking across multiple runs (5-7 runs per participant)
methodology.control_conditions: Comparison of variance between same-rate and mixed-rate conditions

claims.claim_text: Assignment of pulse rate to electrode does not have a consistent effect on pitch ranks across participants, with no clear dominance of either most-apical or most-basal electrode

axis_proposal.definition: Subjective pitch rank on ordinal scale
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Psychophysical measure of perceived pitch height

statistics.note: Comparisons between [1234]SD vs [3214]SD (apical dominance test) and [4321]SD vs [4123]SD (basal dominance test) showed very similar pitch ranks for all participants except M031 and MED05. For MED05, [4321]SD had lower pitch rank, consistent with dominance of pulse rate on electrode 4, but not complete dominance.

methodology.task: Pitch ranking of mixed-rate stimuli with different rate-to-electrode assignments

c3_associations.process: Temporal pitch perception in cochlear implant users, limited by inability to extract F0 from multi-channel temporal fine structure
c3_associations.evidence: CI users cannot extract F0 from harmonically related rates on multiple electrodes. Pitch ranks increase with pulse rate up to 200-300 pps then plateau, consistent with upper limit of temporal pitch. Mixed-rate stimuli produce less reliable pitch than same-rate stimuli.

c3_associations.process: Temporal processing limitations in cochlear implant users, with between-electrode timing affecting pitch perception
c3_associations.evidence: Between-electrode delays significantly affect pitch perception, with long-delay conditions producing higher pitch ranks than short-delay conditions. This suggests that pitch is affected by temporal interactions between electrodes, possibly due to spread of excitation and complex neural responses.

primary_axes.note: Pulse rates tested: 100, 200, 300, 400 pulses per second

r3_coordinates.note: Pitch ranks (DV) increase with pulse rate up to 200-300 pps, then plateau. Mixed-rate stimuli do not produce F0 pitch. Between-electrode delays affect pitch perception.

brain_regions_summary.note: This is a psychophysical study with cochlear implant users. No brain imaging or direct neural recordings. All findings are based on behavioral pitch ranking tasks. The study focuses on cochlear implant electrode stimulation patterns rather than brain regions.

warnings: DV r3_axis unmapped in C01/C04 (axis_proposal provided)
Psychophysical study with cochlear implant users - no brain imaging data
Small sample size (8 participants) with substantial individual differences
Focus on cochlear implant electrode stimulation rather than brain regions

data_quality.note: Psychophysical study with behavioral pitch ranking. Statistics well-reported with p-values and effect sizes (Cohen's d) for main comparisons. R³ coordinates mapped for pulse rate (L4.rate_hz). No brain regions as this is a psychophysical study focusing on cochlear implant stimulation patterns.
